description: Deep dive into object pointers, pointer to members, polymorphism with pointers, virtual functions, dynamic object allocation, and advanced pointer-oop integration.

sections.title: Object Pointers
sections.definition: Pointers can point to objects. Use -> operator to access members. Useful for dynamic allocation and polymorphism.

sections.title: Pointer to Derived Class
sections.definition: Base class pointer can point to derived class object. This enables runtime polymorphism. Derived pointer cannot point to base object.

sections.title: Virtual Functions and Dynamic Binding
sections.definition: Virtual functions allow correct function selection at runtime based on actual object type, not pointer type. Achieved via vtable.

sections.title: Virtual Destructors
sections.definition: Base class destructor should be virtual when deleting derived objects via base pointer. Otherwise, derived destructor not called, causing resource leak.

sections.title: Pointer to Member Functions
sections.definition: Pointers can point to member functions. Syntax: return_type (ClassName::*ptr)(params). Use .* or ->* to invoke.

sections.title: Pointer to Data Members
sections.definition: Pointers can point to member variables. Syntax: type ClassName::*ptr = &ClassName::member.

sections.title: this Pointer in Depth
sections.definition: this is a hidden pointer inside every non-static member function pointing to the current object. Used for chaining, resolving name conflicts, and CRTP.

sections.title: Array of Pointers to Objects
sections.definition: Useful for polymorphic containers: store base class pointers to derived objects.

sections.title: Dynamic Object Creation with new/delete
sections.definition: Objects can be allocated on heap using new, destroyed with delete. new calls constructor, delete calls destructor.

sections.title: Function Pointers vs Virtual Functions
sections.definition: Function pointers can mimic polymorphism but are less flexible. Virtual functions are preferred for OOP.
